content.problemStatement: Your task is to iterate over this string backward and print each character on a new line.
constraints: S consists of printable ASCII characters (including spaces)
content.inputFormat: The first line contains an integer T (1 ≤ T ≤ 10), the number of test cases. Each of the next T lines contains a non‑empty string S (length 1 to 100). The line may contain spaces and any printable ASCII characters.
content.outputFormat: For each test case, output the characters of S in reverse order, one character per line. Test cases are processed sequentially without blank lines between them.

sampleTestCases.output:
e
n
i
h
c
a
M
sampleTestCases.explanation: The single test case string "Machine" reversed yields the characters e, n, i, h, c, a, M, each printed on its own line.
A single‑character string is its own reverse, so the only output line is "a".

sampleTestCases.explanation: First test case: "Hello World" reversed gives d, l, r, o, W, space, o, l, l, e, H. Second test case: "ABC" reversed gives C, B, A.

solution.approach: For each test case read the whole line as a string. Iterate over the string from its last character to the first, printing each character followed by a newline. This directly produces the required reversed order. No additional data structures are needed.
solution.timeComplexity: O(T * L) where L is the length of each string (at most 100). Each character is visited exactly once.
solution.spaceComplexity: O(1) extra space besides the input buffer and output.

code.Python3:
import sys

def main():
    data = sys.stdin.read().splitlines()
    if not data:
        return
    T = int(data[0].strip())
    idx = 1
    out = []
    for _ in range(T):
        s = data[idx] if idx < len(data) else ''
        idx += 1
        out.extend(reversed(s))
    sys.stdout.write('\n'.join(out))

if __name__ == "__main__":
    main()
